Across both elements, participants continued to monitor shipping schedules and sourcing timelines, which can affect near-term availability for buyers. Several companies also cited continuing efforts to secure longer-term feedstock arrangements, aiming to reduce short-term volatility associated with producer lead times and freight costs.

The weekly wrap also highlighted that the market for heavier rare earths remained sensitive to shifts in industrial consumption, particularly from the magnet sector. Where buyers maintained buying plans, price pressure remained limited; where purchases were delayed or consolidated, sellers were more likely to hold their pricing. Market watchers said this pattern contributed to the lack of movement in praseodymium and the steadiness seen in terbium.

“Both praseodymium and terbium showed limited movement this week, with participants largely waiting for clearer indications on end-demand before adjusting pricing,” a Fastmarkets analyst said in the weekly note.

Fastmarkets’ weekly assessments are based on observable market activity, including reported deals, offers, and discussions among buyers and sellers, and they are intended to reflect prevailing market values at the time of the evaluation. The next weekly updates are expected to be published as additional activity emerges and as market participants update their views on supply availability and buying schedules.
bodies_by_headline_hash.ee31e8b743b9d719633f:
MP Materials said it expects second-quarter rare-earth output to stay roughly in line with the prior quarter as the U.S.-based producer navigates seasonal and operational variability at its processing facilities in Nevada and California.

In a quarterly operational update released Wednesday, the company said it is guiding Q2 rare-earth separation output toward the same level as Q1, after reporting first-quarter production that declined from the preceding period. The company produces separated rare earths primarily used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and other industrial applications, with downstream demand tied closely to global manufacturing.

MP Materials’ update focused on rare-earth product output and the progress of ongoing operations at its Mountain Pass site and downstream separation capabilities. The company said it continues to run its separation lines and supply rare-earth concentrate for processing, while managing feedstock availability and throughput based on equipment schedules and inventory movements.

The guidance comes as producers in the rare-earth sector continue to balance ramp-up plans, contract delivery schedules and maintenance requirements. MP Materials has been working to increase reliability and throughput across its value chain, including refining rare-earth concentrate into separated products used by magnet makers.

The company also reiterated that its final output can vary depending on feed quality, chemical processing performance, and unplanned downtime. It said it remains focused on maintaining steady operations and converting inventory into saleable products during the quarter, with production volumes influenced by the timing of shipments.

MP Materials has previously highlighted the importance of separating capacity and recovery rates to determine quarterly results. In earlier updates, the company referenced fluctuations in rare-earth oxide output caused by the underlying chemistry of feed and the stability of separation operations.

“While we plan for steady operations in the second quarter, actual results will depend on operational performance, feed conditions and maintenance activities,” a company spokesperson said in the statement.

Shares of MP Materials have closely tracked updates on rare-earth production and contract milestones, as investors watch for signals on the company’s ability to sustain output in a market still dominated by a small number of processors and magnet supply chains. The company is expected to provide more detailed financial results when it reports second-quarter results later this year.
bodies_by_headline_hash.947fc47fc960eba5d282:
Rare-earth shipments from Malaysia have resumed on a routine schedule after a period of disruption, according to industry contacts tracking export flows of the materials used in magnets and other advanced technologies.

The restart means deliveries of several rare-earth products are again moving at expected intervals through Malaysia’s main export channels, sources said. One trader said paperwork and port clearance processes had been taking longer than normal in recent weeks, contributing to irregular timing for outbound cargoes. The trader said the impact has eased, with vessels now departing within typical lead times.

Malaysia has been an important hub for processing and trading rare-earth compounds and intermediate materials that are later converted into oxides and metals elsewhere. Buyers across the supply chain have been watching shipment timing because downstream manufacturers often keep limited inventories for neodymium, praseodymium and other grades used in electric vehicles, wind turbines and industrial motors.

In the disruption period, some customers reported delayed arrivals and tighter allocations rather than outright cancellations, according to multiple contacts. Shipping documents for certain consignments were submitted later than usual, and the processing of export permits took longer, sources said. Several participants also pointed to demand fluctuations from end-users and adjustments to production planning at processing facilities, which can amplify the effects of administrative delays.

Officials and companies in the supply chain did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the specific cause of the earlier delays. Industry participants said the resumption suggests that administrative bottlenecks and scheduling coordination have been resolved, allowing exporters to return to standard shipping rhythms.

The restart comes as global rare-earth markets remain sensitive to disruptions tied to logistics, regulatory approvals and production changes in major producing regions. While shipment resumption in Malaysia should help stabilize near-term availability for customers sourcing intermediate materials, traders said prices are likely to remain influenced by broader market conditions, including buying patterns and movements in China-linked supply.

For now, market participants said they will continue monitoring outbound freight schedules and inventory levels at receiving ports to confirm whether the improved timing persists in subsequent weeks.
bodies_by_headline_hash.c6d8f15a30d93679a107:
Rare-earth watchers are flagging today’s 30-day moving average as the cleanest snapshot of the market’s recent direction.  
The curve smooths out day-to-day volatility in prices and focuses attention on whether supply conditions are tightening or easing.  
When the moving average tilts upward, buyers typically anticipate firmer availability and higher costs in the weeks ahead.  
A flattening or downward slope often signals softer demand, steadier deliveries, or increased secondary supply.  
Traders use the indicator to time entries ahead of contract windows.  
Producers and buyers watch for crossovers versus prior averages as early warnings of trend shifts.
bodies_by_headline_hash.07a60acdfea1dae348d1:
Rare-earth exchange-traded funds (ETFs) recorded small net outflows over the week as investors trimmed exposure to the sector, according to fund-flow data compiled by Bloomberg.

Across the peer group, investors withdrew a modest net amount, continuing a recent pattern of cautious positioning rather than sustained buying. Flows were mixed among individual products, with some funds seeing inflows while others experienced outflows, resulting in an overall negative weekly total.

The week’s movements reflected broader churn in commodity-linked and metals-related strategies, where allocations often shift in response to near-term price action and inventory signals. In rare earths, investors also weigh the pace of demand recovery and expectations for new supply, which can vary widely across elements used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and electronics.

Fund performance over the week also appeared to influence trading activity. Several issuers with holdings tied to specific rare-earth baskets or related mining and processing companies saw investors rotate between strategies, depending on each fund’s underlying exposure and the currency effects embedded in performance. The resulting rebalancing contributed to the mixed flow picture.

One risk metric tracked by ETF providers—tracking of underlying benchmarks—can tighten when flows are steady and widen when investors frequently enter and exit. Market participants said the small outflows suggest that while some investors reduced exposure, others kept positions aligned with longer-term themes tied to clean energy and industrial manufacturing.

Despite the weekly outflow, analysts said the scale remained limited compared with the size of assets in the funds, implying there was no sign of broad liquidation. Instead, the data pointed to selective adjustments, with investors likely responding to short-term valuation and portfolio needs.

Investors will be watching for additional catalysts, including developments in rare-earth processing capacity, export controls and potential policy changes in major producing countries. With the sector still sensitive to news on supply disruptions and contract pricing, fund flows may continue to fluctuate from week to week.

Additional weekly breakdowns by fund were not immediately available, but the overall net outflow indicated that investor sentiment toward rare-earth exposure remained cautious as the market weighed competing supply and demand signals.
bodies_by_headline_hash.239ed0fb53717bec5f7f:
Chinese technicians guided a Reuters reporter through a rare-earth processing plant in Sichuan, offering a rare look at the steps used to separate, purify and repackage rare-earth materials that are used in magnets, electric vehicles and high-tech electronics.

The facility, located in an industrial zone outside a county town, receives mixed rare-earth concentrates by truck and stores them in covered bays to reduce dust. Workers then feed the material into a series of chemical tanks where it is dissolved and filtered, separating individual rare-earth elements through staged precipitation and solvent extraction processes. Staff said the plant was designed to handle multiple feedstock qualities, using different chemical ratios to adjust for variations in incoming ore.

Near the main processing hall, stainless-steel equipment lines a corridor of pumps, gauges and control panels. Technicians monitor pH levels, temperature and flow rates in real time, while sampling stations collect liquids and slurries at each stage. According to plant staff, quality control teams measure the concentration of target elements and assess impurities before material moves to the next step, to maintain batch consistency for downstream customers.

Environmental controls are integrated across the line, the tour showed. Wastewater passes through neutralisation and treatment systems before discharge, and air-handling units collect and scrub particulates and acid fumes generated during processing. Workers also described the handling of “tailings” and other solid residues, which are stored in lined areas before offsite treatment or utilisation, depending on the waste stream. Company representatives said the facility follows local environmental requirements and internal monitoring protocols.

In an adjacent workshop, employees demonstrated packaging and logistics for rare-earth products, including separated oxides and intermediate compounds in drums and lined bags. Labels list element type, purity specifications and batch identifiers used by customers to qualify materials for magnet and alloy production. The plant also runs testing for moisture and particle size, officials said, to meet specifications for different customer end uses.

Rare-earth processing in China is a critical link in the supply chain for global manufacturers, as separation capacity largely determines access to purified elements. A company representative said the plant’s output is supplied to clients that further convert the material into magnet-ready compounds, while other batches are directed to chemical manufacturing and catalyst applications.
bodies_by_headline_hash.1fd2267c58ca3b4d229e:
A rare-earth industry conference in Perth has concluded with an industry panel that revisited supply risks, refining capacity and demand outlooks for critical minerals used in magnets and clean-energy technologies.

The three-day event brought together producers, buyers, financiers and researchers to discuss the state of global rare-earth supply chains, including the pace of new projects outside China and the bottlenecks that can arise between mine development and the production of separated rare-earth oxides and downstream materials.

Panelists said attention remains focused on security of supply as demand grows for applications such as electric vehicles, wind turbines, and industrial motors, where rare-earth magnets are widely used. Speakers also weighed how long lead times for separation and refining can affect the timing of new material availability, even when upstream mining capacity is expanding.

The group reviewed recent trends in pricing and contracting practices, noting that buyers increasingly seek longer-term supply arrangements to hedge volatility. Participants also discussed efforts to improve traceability and sustainability reporting across mining and processing steps, reflecting growing expectations from governments and end users in major markets.

On the topic of technology, the panel highlighted ongoing work to improve separation efficiencies, reduce waste streams, and increase yields for difficult-to-process elements. Attendees said progress in refining and magnet production outside China remains a central factor for diversifying supply and reducing dependence on a limited number of processing hubs.

One recurring theme was the challenge of scaling rare-earth processing infrastructure. “You can have a deposit, but the supply chain doesn’t truly open until separation and oxide production are in place,” said one industry participant, emphasizing that infrastructure commitments and permitting timelines often determine when new sources reach customers.

Conference organizers said the wrap-up session underscored opportunities for collaboration between companies and governments, particularly around funding models for midstream processing and workforce development. They also pointed to potential gains from industrial partnerships that link mine development with downstream processing and end-use qualification.

The conference ended after a final round of remarks summarizing the main takeaways for stakeholders planning investment and offtake strategies for the next several years, as the sector navigates a balance between expanding capacity and managing market and regulatory uncertainties.
bodies_by_headline_hash.2538146387f234c45dd1:
Shanghai Metals Market (SMM) has released its latest inventory data for rare earths, providing a snapshot of stock levels tracked across key product categories used in magnets, polishing compounds and catalysts.

The dataset covers holdings at locations monitored by SMM, including both commercial inventory and goods held by market participants, and is intended to give traders and end users a view of near-term supply availability. The release follows other regular updates from regional platforms that track movements in rare-earth materials, which remain sensitive to changes in downstream demand and export-linked pricing.

According to SMM’s figures, inventory levels varied by individual rare-earth items, with some categories showing tighter availability while others saw slower drawdowns. The reported changes reflect routine replenishment and consumption patterns in domestic markets, where producers and distributors adjust procurement according to order flow for alloys, permanent magnets and industrial feedstocks.

The update also includes information on mixed rare earths and separation products, which are commonly monitored as proxies for downstream capacity. Market participants typically use these indicators alongside spot quotations and futures activity to gauge whether inventories are building or being absorbed, particularly around periods of seasonal demand and contracting cycles.

SMM said the inventory tracking is based on reported stock positions submitted by participating firms and on standard inventory classification methods used in its reporting process. “The release is compiled from inventory submissions from market participants and reflects the stock status at the reference time,” an SMM representative said in connection with the data format.

Traders said the inventory data could influence short-term sentiment around the rare-earth supply chain, especially for products tied to magnet production, where disruptions can affect lead times for components used in electric vehicles and industrial equipment. The figures are also likely to be watched by processors and manufacturers when planning purchases for the next quarter.

The rare-earth market has seen recurring volatility in recent years, driven by shifts in policy, upstream production schedules and demand from key end-use sectors. As new inventory numbers are incorporated into pricing discussions, the latest SMM release is expected to be referenced in subsequent assessments of supply tightness and restocking needs.
bodies_by_headline_hash.aa9ec2c1313fc2cc3200:
A rare-earth mining equipment firm reported steady quarterly earnings on Monday, citing stable demand for processing machinery used in magnets and other clean-energy applications.

In its results statement, the company said revenue and profitability remained largely in line with the prior year period, supported by repeat orders from customers building or expanding rare-earth processing capacity. The firm did not name any specific clients, but said it continues to supply equipment for separation, refining and material recovery stages used in producing rare-earth metals and downstream products.

Management attributed performance to a relatively balanced order pipeline and improved delivery schedules across several project sites. It said customers have continued to place orders for incremental upgrades to existing plants, including systems that reduce impurities and increase recovery yields. The company also reported that engineering and service revenue contributed to overall stability, reflecting ongoing maintenance contracts.

The firm posted earnings that matched analysts’ expectations, according to two financial analysts who reviewed the company’s filings and estimates. In addition to the earnings figures, the company said it maintained its cost discipline, including tighter procurement and manufacturing planning, which helped offset higher logistics expenses that have affected industrial supply chains.

Looking ahead, the company said it expects demand to remain supported by government-backed incentives for domestic and allied supply chains for rare-earth materials. It also pointed to continued investment in rare-earth magnet production used in electric vehicles, wind power and other sectors, where equipment demand is linked to both capacity additions and technology upgrades.

“We’re seeing steady execution across our customer programs, with follow-on work supporting utilization at our manufacturing sites,” the chief executive officer said in the filing. The executive added that the company remains focused on delivering systems on schedule and on supporting customers through commissioning and ramp-up phases.

The company’s shares were little changed in early trading following the announcement. Its next earnings report is scheduled for later this year, and it said it will provide further details on its backlog, delivery timing and margin outlook in the upcoming quarterly update.
bodies_by_headline_hash.9ba5c63a56c310fc04f2:
A rare-earth recycling startup has raised a Series B round to expand its capacity for recovering critical metals from end-of-life materials and industrial waste, according to the company and its investors.

The startup, which did not disclose the final round size in a public statement, said the funding will be used to build additional processing equipment, secure feedstock supply and increase output of separated rare-earth oxides. Rare earths, which are used in magnets for electric vehicles, wind turbines and defense systems, are often concentrated in a small number of producing countries, driving efforts to develop domestic recycling routes.

The company’s technology centers on separating rare-earth elements from mixed scrap and leaching residues, followed by chemical purification steps designed to produce materials suitable for magnet and alloy supply chains. It said it can handle a range of input streams, including magnet scrap and manufacturing byproducts, as well as certain slags and processing residues where rare-earth content can be recovered.

Industry analysts said recycling is expected to play a larger role as demand for rare-earth magnets rises and the stock of previously sold equipment reaches end-of-life. However, scaling has been constrained by limited collection networks, inconsistent feedstock chemistry and the complexity of separating closely related elements such as neodymium and praseodymium.

The company said it will also invest in workforce and quality systems to meet customer specifications, and plans to expand partnerships with manufacturers that generate scrap from magnet production and electronics. In addition, it intends to pursue new offtake agreements with firms that purchase recycled rare-earth products for downstream processing.

“Series B funding accelerates our path to commercial scale by strengthening our processing capacity and expanding reliable feedstock access,” the company’s chief executive said in a statement. “We are focused on producing consistent, specification-ready rare-earth materials for customers in the magnet and alloy sectors.”

The round follows earlier seed and Series A investment and comes as governments in Europe, the United States and Asia increasingly support recycling initiatives through industrial policy and critical-minerals programs. Investors are also watching for signs that recycled rare earths can compete on both cost and performance, particularly as new magnet production facilities come online.
bodies_by_headline_hash.1431f91aa5b9ee6d4c08:
The export alert circulating today looks bigger than the underlying policy shift.  
Officials have not announced a blanket rare-earth ban across all buyers and routes.  
Instead, the measures focus on a short list of specific grades and processing stages.  
Licensing rules are being tightened, with extra documentation for origin and end use.  
The change appears to target brokers who bundle mixed materials rather than direct producers.  
Customs agencies in several ports will now run additional screening for shipments carrying certain alloy inputs.  
Traders say most legitimate contracts can continue, but lead times may stretch by several weeks.  
Market watchers will watch pricing for dysprosium and neodymium first.
bodies_by_headline_hash.862b0190d4af4c19696f:
If you’ve been losing sleep over a looming dysprosium crisis, take a breath: the “shortage nobody is telling you” framing makes for a great click, but the market reality is more boring—and more fixable. Dysprosium is certainly moving around as supply chains tighten, yet the story isn’t one of sudden depletion or a hidden wall of bad news. It’s the familiar tech-industry problem of uneven lead times, spotty secondary supply, and long procurement cycles colliding with fast-changing demand signals.

Dysprosium’s role in high-performance magnets—especially for EV motors and wind-generation systems—means it’s treated as a strategic input. But Reuters reporting and broader market behavior suggest the bottleneck tends to show up in processing and qualified output, not in an instant disappearance of the element. In other words, the constraint is often where the ore gets refined, separated, and tested at scale, then locked into contracts. That’s why “shortage” can look dramatic even when the underlying issue is procedural: capacity ramps slowly, qualification timelines drag, and inventories get rebalanced across regions.

There’s also a subtle dynamic that headline writers tend to flatten. When prices spike or forecasts get noisy, downstream buyers frequently front-load orders, which temporarily increases apparent strain. Then, as new batches clear the pipeline or alternative compositions get approved, the market cools—even if nothing “shocking” was uncovered. Add in policy shifts, transport disruptions, and the fact that dysprosium is often produced alongside other rare-earths, and you get volatility that can look like conspiracy-level secrecy when it’s really industrial scheduling.

So what should you actually watch? Less doom, more procurement plumbing: medium-term refining capacity additions, inventory drawdowns at magnet producers, and any credible moves toward recycling rare-earth magnets. The next few quarters will likely reveal whether this is a cyclical squeeze or a structural one—and if the industry plays its cards right, the “truth” may be that dysprosium supply is manageable when planning starts earlier than the headlines.
bodies_by_headline_hash.bef1a1f7410b46702125:
For years, the rare-earth story has been told like a looming disaster: scarce materials, geopolitical brinkmanship, and supply chains held hostage by a handful of producers. But that narrative is too convenient—and increasingly inaccurate. The rare-earth crisis, at least in its most alarmist form, is overblown.

To be clear, the risks are real. Rare-earth magnets are essential to wind turbines, electric vehicles, and many components of modern defense and aerospace systems. Concentrated processing capacity and trade restrictions can still create genuine bottlenecks. Yet “crisis” implies an imminent collapse of supply or an unavoidable economic catastrophe. Most of what we have instead is a managed transition—one that has already moved from emergency footing to industrial planning.

Consider what has changed. Downstream demand forecasts are no longer built on fantasy assumptions; they incorporate efficiency gains, alternative chemistries, and gradual capacity additions. Companies have invested in “design for supply,” meaning they are reshaping products to use less material, substitute less critical inputs, and qualify new suppliers more quickly. Meanwhile, mining and separation projects that once sat perpetually “in the pipeline” have advanced from announcement to permitting, construction, and commissioning. Even slow progress matters when the market is simply trying to avoid a sudden shortage.

There is also a political dimension that tends to get flattened in doom reports. Governments are diversifying procurement, financing stockpiles, and supporting domestic refining. These steps may not eliminate dependency overnight, but they do reduce the probability that a single disruption cascades into a systemic shock. A supply chain that is resilient is not one that is perfectly diversified; it is one that can absorb stress without failing.

Still, skeptics will ask: if the crisis is overblown, why do prices and headlines still flare? Because markets are forward-looking and sensitive to expectations. A pause in one plant, a delayed shipment, or a temporary policy signal can lift prices even when the underlying medium-term outlook is stabilizing. Volatility is not the same thing as scarcity.

My bottom line is this: the rare-earth market has challenges, but the most dramatic narratives exaggerate the timeline and underestimate adaptation. The real story is not panic—it is industrial adjustment. Policymakers and investors should keep pushing for diversification and processing capacity, but they should also stop treating every fluctuation as proof of an impending breakdown.
bodies_by_headline_hash.f992e9f34fab5c25b2c7:
For years, critical minerals policy has been treated like an industrial hygiene problem: secure supply, reduce risk, diversify sources, repeat. But that approach—focused mainly on procurement and leverage—has left us with a blind spot. We’re still trying to solve a political problem with procurement tools, as if the market will reward good intentions and transparent contracts in the absence of strategic power.

I don’t deny the urgency. Rare earths, lithium, graphite, nickel, and copper sit at the heart of electrification, grid buildouts, defense systems, and the next generation of manufacturing. When supply chains tighten, the costs don’t stay in industrial spreadsheets; they show up in higher bills, delayed projects, and fragile national capabilities. However, urgency is not an excuse for narrow policy.

The question we should be asking is simple: are we building resilience—or merely swapping one dependency for another? If the goal is resilience, then “rethink” must go beyond telling industries to find alternative suppliers. It must also confront the incentive structure that determines where value is captured, how quickly projects move from permitting to production, and which communities bear the environmental burden.

Right now, too many critical-minerals strategies are built on the assumption that downstream demand will automatically pull upstream investment. That assumption is increasingly wrong. Projects can take a decade to permit and finance, while capital markets price uncertainty and regulators demand standards that vary widely across borders. Meanwhile, competitors can use state-backed procurement, strategic stockpiles, and export relationships to lock in capacity before Western partners have even agreed on a contract.

So my argument is straightforward: policy should focus on building domestic and allied processing capacity—not just mining—and on coordinating the rules that govern permitting, environmental review, and labor. Separation and refining matter because they determine whether a country merely ships raw material or actually controls the choke points where performance and price are set. That is where policy can create durable capability.

At the same time, we need a more honest sustainability framework. If we want critical minerals to power clean energy, they must be produced in ways that communities accept and regulators enforce. Short-term “fast tracking” without credible standards will backfire politically and socially, and it will undermine the very permitting timelines the industry says it needs.

Finally, we should treat critical minerals policy as industrial policy in the real sense: using credit, offtake, and procurement to de-risk projects that deliver both economic value and security. Done well, this approach reduces volatility, accelerates learning curves, and strengthens bargaining power.

The choice is not between market forces and government intervention. It’s between targeted, accountable state action and another round of reactive scrambling when the next supply shock hits.
bodies_by_headline_hash.fb01b364183bfe33a5f4: China has halted exports of dysprosium, a rare-earth metal used in magnets and defense-related systems, as it subjects shipments to a security review, according to trade documents and notices circulated to exporters. The suspension began in early March 2026, with some consignments reportedly held at Chinese ports pending clarification of end-use documentation and destination screening. Dysprosium shipments had been moving under a patchwork of export licensing requirements, but regulators have tightened checks following updated national security guidance published late February. The review process is expected to affect both spot and contract deliveries, with some suppliers warning of multi-week delays. Market participants said the move could exacerbate tight global availability ahead of the second quarter, when demand from electric vehicle drivetrains, wind turbines and high-performance motors is typically strongest. Prices for heavy rare earths have already been volatile since mid-2025, and analysts forecast further dislocations if the export stoppage extends beyond April.
bodies_by_headline_hash.8d6cb5768493f1803b43:
Beijing has confirmed it will halt exports of dysprosium, a key rare earth metal used in high-performance magnets for electric vehicles and wind turbines, according to a government notice made public on Sunday.

The measure, announced by Chinese authorities in a document posted late Saturday, indicates that shipments of dysprosium outside the country will be suspended while regulators review compliance and supply arrangements. The notice does not specify the start date or a firm end date, but it points to the suspension being administered through customs and export licensing channels.

China is the world’s largest producer of dysprosium and other heavy rare earth elements, with a significant share of global processing capacity. Dysprosium is widely used to boost the heat resistance and performance of neodymium-iron-boron magnets, which underpin motors and generators across industrial and clean-energy supply chains.

Markets have been watching China’s rare earth export policy for months as Beijing balances export controls with domestic demand and strategic stockpiling. The government’s latest confirmation follows a period in which companies and buyers reported tighter paperwork requirements for some rare earth products, prompting concerns over potential disruption in downstream manufacturing.

In its notice, China cited the need to strengthen management of rare earth exports and ensure the “orderly” flow of materials. It also instructs that approved export permits remain subject to “relevant regulations,” suggesting that any resumed shipments would require renewed authorizations and documentation.

The announcement is expected to affect importers and magnet producers outside China, particularly those relying on steady dysprosium inputs for bonded and sintered magnet production. Companies typically seek inventory buffers ahead of policy shifts, but lead times for rare earth supply contracts can extend for months due to refining and separation capacity constraints.

Analysts said the immediate impact will depend on how buyers interpret the scope of the halt, including whether it applies to all forms of dysprosium products and to shipments already in transit. Some industry participants maintain that China may allow limited exports under special licensing arrangements, while others said the confirmation signals a broader tightening of controls.

A spokesperson for China’s rare earth regulators did not provide additional details in the public notice beyond the suspension directive, Reuters reported.
bodies_by_headline_hash.138f58d67ecd46f76b19:
Vietnam’s government has revoked export licences for rare earths, a decision that tightens controls on the country’s access to minerals used in electronics and defence supply chains, according to a cabinet resolution issued this week.

The cabinet, in the resolution dated March 13, ordered relevant ministries to withdraw licences that had been issued under prior regulations and to suspend related export activities. The move applies to licences covering the mining and export of rare earth products, with the government citing the need to align permissions with updated management requirements.

Officials said the revocation is intended to improve oversight of rare earth operations and to prevent exports outside the scope of approved planning. The resolution directs agencies to review compliance by licence holders and to ensure that any remaining activities meet the conditions set by current rules.

The government also instructed provincial authorities to coordinate with central ministries on monitoring, including tracking production and export documentation. Companies holding revoked licences are required to stop exporting the covered materials unless they receive new approvals under the revised framework.

Vietnam has expanded its role in materials processing and extraction as global demand for rare earth magnets, catalysts and other industrial inputs has risen. The country is not known as a top global rare earth producer compared with China, but it has been developing related projects and has sought to increase value-added processing capacity.

The cabinet resolution comes amid broader international pressure to strengthen transparency in critical minerals and reduce supply-chain risk. Vietnam’s rare earth management has previously been the subject of regulatory scrutiny, particularly around licensing, export permissions and environmental compliance.

A government representative said the decision reflects “the updated state management of rare earth exports and the need to ensure licences comply fully with current regulations.”

It was not immediately clear how many licences were affected or whether any exports were already shipped under the revoked authorisations. The cabinet said ministries will provide further guidance on procedures for licence reapplication, and on how businesses can transition to the new approval process if they wish to continue exporting rare earth products.
